question: Q5: Give one example each from the Indian market respectively for - a) Orphan brand b) Identifying neglected segments

model_answer.answer_text:
**a) Orphan Brand — Gold Spot**

An orphan brand is a once-popular brand with diminished equity that a parent company allows to decline by withdrawing marketing support (Keller & Swaminathan, Ch.14). **Gold Spot**, launched by Parle in 1952, was India’s leading orange soda for decades with the iconic tagline “The Zing Thing.” In 1993, Parle sold Gold Spot along with Thums Up, Limca, and Maaza to Coca-Cola for $40 million. Coca-Cola then deliberately discontinued Gold Spot by 2000 to make way for its global brand Fanta — a textbook case of a parent company orphaning an acquired brand. Despite its withdrawal, Gold Spot retains nostalgic equity among Indian consumers who grew up with it (“Gold Spot was withdrawn by Coke from the market in order to re-make space for Coca-Cola’s Fanta brand” — Wikipedia).

**b) Identifying Neglected Segments — CavinKare Chik Shampoo**

Identifying neglected segments means finding underserved customer groups and targeting them to rebuild or grow a brand (Keller & Swaminathan, Ch.14). **CavinKare’s Chik Shampoo** is a powerful Indian example: in the 1990s, rural consumers washed their hair with soap because existing shampoo brands were priced for urban middle-class buyers. Founder CK Ranganathan identified this neglected rural segment and launched Chik shampoo in Re 1 sachets — the first shampoo sachet in India — making the product affordable for daily-wage earners. This sachet innovation targeted “the rural consumer who had so far been ignored by the then MNCs” (ThePrint, 2022). By 2003, Chik captured 22.49% of India’s shampoo market by volume, proving that identifying and serving neglected segments can unlock massive growth.
model_answer.framework: Keller Ch.14 — Managing Brands Over Time (Orphan Brands + Brand Revitalization via Neglected Segments)

framework_source.section: Chapter 14, pp.536-538 (orphan brand) and pp.484-486 (neglected segments / revitalization)
framework_source.cite_as: Keller & Swaminathan, Strategic Brand Management, 5th Ed, Ch.14
